elements.sent-2411-003: There has been a growing interest in enhancing rule-based agent-based models (ABMs) for social media platforms (i.e., X, Reddit) with more realistic large language model~(LLM) agents, thereby allowing for a more nuanced study of complex systems.

elements.sent-2411-008: Additionally, \GENSS supports large-scale user simulations, capable of modeling up to one million users.

elements.sent-2411-011: Moreover, we provide observations of social phenomena at different agent group scales.

elements.sent-2411-014: Complex societal systems (e.g., social media, cities, ecosystems, and financial markets) are characterized by many interconnected and interdependent components or agents.
elements.sent-2411-015: These interactions give rise to emergent behaviors that cannot be predicted by analyzing the actions of individual alone \citep{ladyman2013complex .}
elements.sent-2411-016: These systems are important in the increasingly digital world we live in, but conducting experiments with complex systems can be very costly in terms of time and resources.
elements.sent-2411-017: Therefore, scientists have often relied on mathematical or agent-based models (ABMs) to understand, analyze, or predict phenomena and outcomes that are difficult or impossible to conduct real-world experiments (e.g., misinformation propagation \citep{gausen2022using , online polarization \citep{song2017dynamic , and herd effect \citep{lee2015heterogeneous ).}}}

elements.sent-2411-021: Recently, large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capability to mimic human behaviors~\citep{park2022simulacra, park2023generative, zhou2023sotopia, wang2023user, gao2023s3, mou2024unveiling .}

elements.sent-2411-023: To develop and evaluate these LLM agents, researchers will need to move beyond standard benchmarks by defining social situations and distinct personas, as well as integrating these agents into simulated platforms or sandbox environments for more comprehensive testing and analysis~\citep{park2023generative .}

elements.sent-2411-033: To this end, we propose \GENSS, a collection of generalizable and scalable ABMs to simulate a wide variety of phenomena in various social media platforms.
elements.sent-2411-034: How \GENSS works and why \GENSS is generalizable? \GENSS is built upon five foundational components, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:intro-workflow , including the Environment Server, RecSys, Agent Module, Time Engine, and Scalable Inferencer.}

elements.sent-2411-039: These actions then update the environment's state in real-time.

elements.sent-2411-043: Still, the importance of the scale of ABMs remains largely under-explored in existing literature.
elements.sent-2411-044: Our findings demonstrate that increasing the number of agents is crucial for accurately simulating group behavior and making user perspectives more valuable and diverse.
elements.sent-2411-045: To facilitate these large-scale simulations, we develop a comprehensive user generation method that enables extensive agent experiments, along with an advanced multi-processing technique to efficiently handle high-demand inference requests.
elements.sent-2411-046: Additionally, the RecSys allows agents to access information of personal interest from a large volume of data, thereby facilitating more structured and organized large-scale interactions.
elements.sent-2411-047: To validate the effectiveness of \GENSS, we replicate various social phenomena (such as information spreading, group polarization, and the herd effect) across different platforms (X and Reddit).
elements.sent-2411-048: The experimental results indicate that \GENSS can closely replicate phenomena and outcomes observed in human society, including trends in information spreading, the increasing polarization of agent opinions within the interaction, and the herd effect among agents.
elements.sent-2411-049: Additionally, we also observe unique phenomena within agent societies, such as more severe group polarization in uncensored LLMs and agents being more susceptible to the herd effect compared to humans.
elements.sent-2411-050: Furthermore, we find that the number of agents plays a significant role in simulating group behavior as well as in the diversity and helpfulness of agents' opinions.
elements.sent-2411-051: We hope that \GENSS will support research across various disciplines and contribute to the future study of agent-based societies.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figures/method.pdf} \caption{The workflow of \GENSS. During the registration phase, real-world or generated user information will be registered on the Environment Server. The Time Engine manages the agents' temporal behaviors, while the Scalable Inference handles large-scale inference requests from users. } \label{fig:intro-workflow}
elements.sent-2411-052: In this section, we describe the workflow and critical internal mechanisms of \GENSS, which enable it to be easily generalized and scaled to support the simulation of millions of LLM-based agents.
elements.sent-2411-053: During the registration phase, \GENSS requires users' information, including name, self-description, and historical posts.
elements.sent-2411-054: After registration, each user~(or agent) receives a character description and an action description, guiding them to better align with their characteristics and to perform specific actions on various social media platforms.
elements.sent-2411-055: In the simulation phase, the environment sends user-related information—such as the user’s past behavior and self-description to the RecSys.
elements.sent-2411-056: The RecSys filters posts from the environment and suggests posts that are likely to be of interest to the agent.
elements.sent-2411-057: Based on these posts, the agent’s self-description, and other contextual factors, the agent selects actions to take, such as liking or reposting a post.
elements.sent-2411-058: Chain-of-Thought (CoT, \citet{wei2022chain ) reasoning is incorporated, enabling the agent to generate reasoning alongside its actions.}
elements.sent-2411-059: The agent’s activation is governed by the time engine, which stores the user’s hourly activity probability in a 24-dimension list.
elements.sent-2411-060: Based on these usage patterns, the time engine probabilistically activates the agent at specific times.
elements.sent-2411-061: After the agent performs actions, the results are updated in the environment server.
elements.sent-2411-062: For example, newly created posts are added to the post table in the database, or the user's relations network is updated when they follow a new user.
elements.sent-2411-063: These updates ensure that the environment accurately reflects the most recent state of the user's social network.
elements.sent-2411-064: The role of the environment server is to maintain the status and data of social media platforms, such as users' information, posts, and user relationships.
elements.sent-2411-065: We implement the environment server using a relational database to manage and store this information efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-066: The detailed database structure is provided in the appendix~\ref{appendix:architecture .}
elements.sent-2411-067: The environment server is primarily composed of six components: users, posts, comments, relations, traces, and recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-068: The user table stores basic information about each user, such as their name and biography.
elements.sent-2411-069: The post table and the comment table each contain all the posts and comments made on the platform, including detailed information like the number of likes and the creation time.
elements.sent-2411-070: The relations component comprises multiple tables that store various types of relationships, such as follow and mutual relationships between users, likes between users and posts, among others.
elements.sent-2411-071: Each user's entire action history is recorded in the trace table.
elements.sent-2411-072: The recommendation table is populated by the output of the RecSys after analyzing the user’s trace table.
elements.sent-2411-073: The database can be dynamically updated.
elements.sent-2411-074: For example, new users, posts, comments, and follow relationships can be added over time.
elements.sent-2411-075: This dynamic flexibility significantly enhances the versatility and usability of \GENSS.
elements.sent-2411-076: The role of the RecSys is to control the information seen by agents, playing a crucial part in shaping the information flow.
elements.sent-2411-077: We develop RecSys for two popular social media platforms: X and Reddit.
elements.sent-2411-078: In-network content is ranked by popularity (likes) before recommendation.
elements.sent-2411-079: Out-of-network posts, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:recsys_out_network , are recommended based on interest matching using TwHIN-BERT \citep{zhang2023twhinbert , which models user interests based on profiles and recent activities by vectors' similarity.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Factors like recency (prioritizing newer posts) and the number of followers of the post's creator (simulating superuser broadcasting) are also taken into account to recommend relevant out-of-network posts, details are presented in Appendix \ref{appendix: recsys appendix .}
elements.sent-2411-081: Additionally, the post count from in-network and out-of-network sources can be adjusted to suit different scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-082: For Reddit, the RecSys is modeled based on Reddit’s disclosed post ranking algorithm \citep{amir2015 , which calculates a hot score to prioritize posts.}
elements.sent-2411-083: This score integrates likes, dislikes, and created time, ensuring that the most recent and popular posts are ranked at the top, while those less popular or controversial rank lower.
elements.sent-2411-084: Specifically, the calculation formula is: \begin{equation}h = \log_{10}\left(\max\left(\left|u - d\right|, 1\right)\right) + \mathrm{sign}(u-d) \cdot \frac{t - t_0}{45000}\end{equation} where $h$ indicates the hot score, $u$ represents the number of upvotes, $d$ represents the number of downvotes, and $t$ is the submission time in seconds since the Unix epoch, $t_0 = 1134028003$.
elements.sent-2411-085: We rank the posts based on hot scores to identify the top $k$ posts for recommendation, with the number of recommended posts (i.e., $k$) varying depending on the experiment; further details are presented in Appendix \ref{appendix: herd effect setting .}
elements.sent-2411-086: Our agent module is based on large language models, and the core features of the agent module are inherited from CAMEL \citep{li2023camel .}
elements.sent-2411-087: The agent module consists primarily of a memory module and an action module.
elements.sent-2411-088: The memory module stores information the agent has encountered.
elements.sent-2411-089: To help the agent better understand its role when performing actions, the memory includes sufficient information about posts, e.g. the number of likes, comments, and the likes on comments.
elements.sent-2411-090: Additionally, it stores the user’s previous actions and the reasoning behind them.
elements.sent-2411-091: The action module enables 21 different types of interactions with the environment, including sign up, refresh, trend, search posts, search users, create post, repost, follow, unfollow, mute, like, unlike, dislike, undo dislike, unmute, create comment, like comment, unlike comment, dislike comment, undo dislike comment, and do nothing.
elements.sent-2411-092: The details of these actions are available in the Appendix~\ref{appendix:action_prompt .}
elements.sent-2411-093: We also utilize CoT reasoning to enhance the interpretability of the agent behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-094: By incorporating a larger action space, we increase user interaction diversity, making them closer to real-world social media platforms.
elements.sent-2411-095: It is crucial to incorporate temporal features into the agent's simulation to accurately reflect how their real-world identities influence online behavior patterns.
elements.sent-2411-096: To address this, we define each agent's hourly activity level based on historical interaction frequency or customized settings.
elements.sent-2411-097: Each agent is initialized with a 24-dimensional vector representing the probability of activity in each hour.
elements.sent-2411-098: The simulation environment activates agents based on these probabilities, rather than activating all agents simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-099: Moreover, we manage time progression within the simulation environment using a time step approach (i.e., one time step is equal to 3 minutes in \GENSS), similar to the approach used in \citet{park2023generative , which accommodates varying LLM inference speeds across different setups.}
elements.sent-2411-100: Additionally, since the creation time of a post within a single time step is crucial for the Reddit recommendation system, we propose an alternative time-flow setting.
elements.sent-2411-101: This setting linearly maps real-world time using a scale factor to adjust the simulation time, ensuring that actions executed earlier within the same time step are recorded with earlier timestamps in the database.
elements.sent-2411-102: We design a highly concurrent distributed system where agents, the environment server, and inference services operate as independent modules, exchanging data through information communication channels.
elements.sent-2411-103: The system leverages asynchronous mechanisms to allow agents to send multiple requests concurrently, even while waiting for responses from previous interactions, and the environment module processes incoming messages in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-104: Inference services manage GPU resources through a dedicated manager, which balances agent requests across available GPUs to ensure efficient resource utilization.
elements.sent-2411-106: The user generation algorithm addresses platform constraints and privacy concerns by combining real user data with a relationship network model, simulating up to one million users while preserving the scale-free nature of social networks.
elements.sent-2411-107: It generates diverse user profiles based on population distributions, simplifying dimensions like age, personality, and profession as independent variables.
elements.sent-2411-108: Core and ordinary users are linked into a network using interest-based sampling, with a 0.2 probability of following core users, ensuring diversity and preventing network density.
elements.sent-2411-109: Details are presented in Appendix \ref{appendix:misinformation user generation , \ref{appendix:group polarization user generation and \ref{appendix: herd effect dataset .}}}

elements.subsection-2411-009: Can \GENSS be Adapted to Various Platforms and Scenarios to Replicate Real-world Phenomena?

elements.sent-2411-143: Finding 1: \GENSS can replicate the information spreading process in the real world in terms of scale and maximum breadth without evident offset; however, the depth trend is smaller compared to real-world trends.
elements.sent-2411-144: We compare the simulation information propagation process with the real-world ground truth in Figure~\ref{fig:simu_and_real_mean .}
elements.sent-2411-145: Overall, the \GENSS simulation results align with real-world information dissemination trends well, with an error margin of normalized RMSE around 30\%.
elements.sent-2411-146: This validates \GENSS's effectiveness in modeling these dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-147: However, we observe that the depth of \GENSS simulation propagation is smaller than the real-world propagation in Figure \ref{fig:simu_and_real_mean .}
elements.sent-2411-148: This discrepancy likely arises from the complexity and precision of real-world RecSys and user profiles.
elements.sent-2411-149: While our RecSys effectively captures the broadcasting effect of super users, data limitations hinder its ability to accurately represent nuanced user profiles.
elements.sent-2411-150: As a result, the simplified design of our RecSys struggles to model intermediary users with the same level of precision.
elements.sent-2411-151: Finding 2: \GENSS can replicate the phenomenon of group polarization, where opinions become increasingly extreme during information propagation.
elements.sent-2411-152: This effect is even more pronounced in uncensored models.
elements.sent-2411-153: Studying how users' opinions evolve during information propagation is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-154: Here, we examine group polarization during information propagation.
elements.sent-2411-155: Group Polarization occurs when individuals with similar views adopt more extreme positions after exchanging opinions.
elements.sent-2411-156: For example, a group with moderately conservative views may become more conservative through interaction.

elements.subsection-2411-010: Does the Number of Agents Affect the Accuracy of Simulating Group Behavior?
elements.sent-2411-171: A natural question to ask is how an increasing number of agents might influence group polarization and individual user opinions.
elements.sent-2411-172: Therefore, we conduct experiments on group polarization at different agent scales i.e., from 196 to 100K.
elements.sent-2411-173: To investigate how the same agents' opinions change across different scales, we collect suggestions from the same 196 users in all experiments.
elements.sent-2411-174: The other experimental settings are kept consistent with those described in group polarization.
elements.sent-2411-175: We run the simulation for 30 time steps.
elements.sent-2411-176: We visualize the distribution of agents' opinions at different scales using Nomic Atlas~\citep{Nomic , as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:group_polar_scale .}}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{figures/different_scale.pdf} \caption{Visualization of 196 core users' opinions across different scale of agents and the evaluation results of helpfulness. } \label{fig:group_polar_scale}
elements.sent-2411-177: Finding 4: Larger group leads to more helpful and diverse responses.
elements.sent-2411-178: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:group_polar_scale , we find that when the number of agents increases from 196 to 10,196, there is a significant enhancement in the diversity of user opinions.}
elements.sent-2411-179: Additionally, following the evaluation criteria from Safe-RLHF \citep{dai2023safe , we assess which set of user opinions—those from 196 or 10,196 agents—is more helpful.}

elements.subsection-2411-011: Misinformation Spreading in One Million Agents
elements.sent-2411-191: Understanding the spread of true and false information is critical for combating misinformation and mitigating its influence on public opinion and behavior.
elements.sent-2411-192: To explore this, we designed a large-scale simulation of a social media platform resembling Twitter, with one million users.
elements.sent-2411-193: Within this environment, celebrity accounts with large followings were used to disseminate four distinct information sets, consisting of both factual and false narratives related to technology, health, education, and entertainment.
elements.sent-2411-194: Details are presented in~\ref{appendix:misinfo and truth pairs .}
elements.sent-2411-195: We analyze the dynamics of how both true and false information spread among users in this large-scale simulation.
elements.sent-2411-196: In this experiment, the activation probability—which defines the likelihood of users sharing information—is set at 0.1 for core users and 0.01 for regular users.
elements.sent-2411-197: We visualize the changes in the number of posts related to both rumors and truthful information over time, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:misinfo .}
elements.sent-2411-198: Our findings indicate that rumors have a significantly broader reach than truthful information, which suggests that agents in the simulation tend to engage more with rumor posts that are novel and emotionally provocative~\citep{vosoughi2018spread .}
elements.sent-2411-199: We also use \href{https://neo4j.com/ {Neo4j to visualize the evolving social connections formed by agents during interactions in Figure~\ref{fig:1m_follows , observing a tendency for users to form isolated groups over time.}}}

elements.lstlisting-2411-001: # OBJECTIVE You're a Twitter/Reddit user, and I'll present you with some posts. After you see the posts, choose some actions from the following functions. - sign_up: Signs up a new user with the provided username, name, and bio. - Arguments: "user_name" (str): The username for the new user. "name" (str): The full name of the new user. "bio" (str): A brief biography of the new user. - create_post: Create a new post with the given content. - Arguments: "content" (str): The content of the post to be created. - repost: Repost a post. - Arguments: "post_id" (integer) - The ID of the post to be reposted. You can `repost` when you want to spread it. - like_post: Likes a specified post. - Arguments: "post_id" (integer) - The ID of the post to be liked. You can `like` when you feel something interesting or you agree with. - unlike_post: Removes a previous like from a post. - Arguments: "post_id" (int): The ID of the post from which to remove the like. You can `unlike` when you reconsider your stance or if the like was made unintentionally. - dislike_post: Dislikes a specified post. - Arguments: "post_id" (integer) - The ID of the post to be disliked. You can use `dislike` when you disagree with a post or find it uninteresting. - undo_dislike_post: Removes a previous dislike from a post. - Arguments: "post_id" (int): The ID of the post from which to remove the dislike. You can `undo_dislike` when you change your mind or if the dislike was made by mistake. - create_comment: Creates a comment on a specified post to engage in conversations or share your thoughts on a post. - Arguments: "post_id" (integer) - The ID of the post to comment on. "content" (str) - The content of the comment. - like_comment: Likes a specified comment. - Arguments: "comment_id" (integer) - The ID of the comment to be liked. Use `like_comment` to show agreement or appreciation for a comment. - unlike_comment: Removes a previous like from a comment. - Arguments: "comment_id" (integer) - The ID of the comment from which to remove the like. Use `unlike_comment` when you change your opinion about the comment or if the like was made by accident. - dislike_comment: Dislikes a specified comment. - Arguments: "comment_id" (integer) - The ID of the comment to be disliked. Use `dislike_comment` when you disagree with a comment or find it unhelpful. - undo_dislike_comment: Removes a previous dislike from a comment. - Arguments: "comment_id" (integer) - The ID of the comment from which to remove the dislike. Use `undo_dislike_comment` when you reconsider your initial reaction or if the dislike was made unintentionally. - follow: Follow a user specified by 'followee_id'. You can `follow' when you respect someone, love someone, or care about someone. - Arguments: "followee_id" (integer) - The ID of the user to be followed. - unfollow: Stops following a user. - Arguments: "followee_id" (int): The user ID of the user to stop following. - mute: Mute a user specified by 'mutee_id'. You can `mute' when you hate someone, dislike someone, or disagree with someone. - Arguments: "mutee_id" (integer) - The ID of the user to be muted. - unmute: Unmute a user specified by 'mutee_id'. You can unmute when you decide to stop ignoring their content or wish to see their messages and posts again. - Arguments: "mutee_id" (integer) - The ID of the user to be unmuted. - search_posts: Searches for posts based on specified criteria. - Arguments: "query" (str) - The search query to find relevant posts. Use `search_posts` to explore posts related to specific topics or hashtags. - search_user: Searches for a user based on specified criteria. - Arguments: "query" (str) - The search query to find relevant users. Use `search_user` to find profiles of interest or to explore their posts. - trend: Retrieves the current trending topics. - No arguments required. Use `trend` to stay updated with what's currently popular or being widely discussed on the platform. - refresh: Refreshes the feed to get the latest posts. - No arguments required. Use `refresh` to update your feed with the most recent posts - do_nothing: Most of the time, you just don't feel like reposting or liking a post, and you just want to look at it. In such cases, choose this action "do_nothing" # SELF-DESCRIPTION Your actions should be consistent with your self-description and personality. {description} # RESPONSE FORMAT Your answer should follow the response format: {{ "reason": "your feeling about these posts and users, then choose some functions based on the feeling. Reasons and explanations can only appear here.", "functions": [{{ "name": "Function name 1", "arguments": {{ "argument_1": "Function argument", "argument_2": "Function argument" }} }}, {{ "name": "Function name 2", "arguments": {{ "argument_1": "Function argument", "argument_2": "Function argument" }} }}] }}) }} Ensure that your output can be directly converted into **JSON format**, and avoid outputting anything unnecessary! elements.sent-2411-272: The recommendation system ranks all posts and saves the highest-ranked ones in a recommendation table within the database.
elements.sent-2411-273: The size of this table can be adjusted, though it remains the same for all users during a given experiment.
elements.sent-2411-274: When an agent selects the refresh action, the environment server retrieves the post IDs linked to the user's ID from the recommendation table.
elements.sent-2411-275: A subset of these post IDs is then randomly sampled, and the environment server queries the post table to retrieve the full content of the corresponding posts, which are then sent to the user.
elements.sent-2411-276: The recommendation algorithm used in X can be summarized by the following formula, which calculates the score between a post and a user. \begin{equation}\text{Score} = R \times F \times S\end{equation} where: \begin{equation}R = \ln\left(\frac{271.8 - (t_{\text{current}} - t_{\text{created}})}{100}\right)\end{equation} \begin{equation}F = \max\left(1, \log_{1000}(\text{fan count} + 1)\right)\end{equation} \begin{equation}S = \text{cosine similarity} \left(E_p, E_u\right)\end{equation} In this context:.
elements.sent-2411-277: Information Channel: During social simulations, multiple agents asynchronously and concurrently interact with both the social media environment and the inference management servers.
elements.sent-2411-278: To facilitate this, the server utilizes an advanced event-driven architecture that broadens event categories to encompass various agent actions and large model inference requests.
elements.sent-2411-279: Communications between the agents and the servers are facilitated through a dedicated channel.
elements.sent-2411-280: This channel comprises an asynchronous message queue to receive agent requests and a thread-safe dictionary for response storage.
elements.sent-2411-281: Upon receiving a request message from an agent, the information channel automatically assigns a UUID to ensure traceability.
elements.sent-2411-282: After processing the request, the server stores the response in the dictionary, using the UUID as the key.
elements.sent-2411-284: Inference Manager: The manager within the inference service is capable of managing GPU devices.
elements.sent-2411-285: This enables our system to flexibly scale the number of graphics cards up or down.
elements.sent-2411-286: Additionally, the manager can distribute inference requests from agents as evenly as possible across all graphics cards for processing, thereby ensuring the efficient utilization of GPU resources.
elements.sent-2411-287: We randomly select 198 propagations from \citet{twitter15dataset and \citet{twitter16dataset , Each propagation dataset provides the source post's posting time, post content, and the propagation tree, with each node containing the user ID, repost ID, and repost time.}}
elements.sent-2411-288: We first use the user IDs from the propagation tree to retrieve the corresponding user's profile, the following list, and previous posts.
elements.sent-2411-289: The time period for retrieving previous posts is set to three days before the source post's posting.
elements.sent-2411-290: It is important to note that due to the high cost of data collection, we only collect posts from specific time periods within these three days, such as the hour before the source post's posting and the two hours following the source post's posting each day.
elements.sent-2411-291: Posts from the hour before the source post's posting are included in the simulation as extra noise to simulate real-world conditions better.
elements.sent-2411-292: Furthermore, since user profiles contain only basic descriptions, we would prompt GPT-3.5 Turbo to generate more detailed user profiles based on the user profiles and all previous posts.
elements.sent-2411-293: The recommendation system would use this detailed profile to create a richer user representation.

elements.sent-2411-375: The current recommendation system is only designed at a high level similar to platforms like X (formerly Twitter) or Reddit.
elements.sent-2411-376: For example, the RecSys designed following X's model only recommends semantically similar posts based on the user's profile and recent activity.
elements.sent-2411-377: More complex recommendation algorithms, such as collaborative filtering, have not been implemented in \GENSS, leading to a misalignment between \GENSS's performance and real-world propagation data.
elements.sent-2411-378: Whether we obtain user data through the Twitter API or the User Generation algorithm proposed in \GENSS, both approaches abstract the real individual to some extent, leading to a natural gap between our simulator and the real world.
elements.sent-2411-379: Although we have expanded the action space on social media platforms to a considerable extent, not all possible actions are covered.
elements.sent-2411-380: For example, our platform currently does not support features like bookmarking, tipping, purchasing, or live streaming, which could be added in future work.
elements.sent-2411-381: Additionally, the current simulation operates solely in a text-based environment, meaning agents are unable to perceive images, videos, or audio.
elements.sent-2411-382: Future extensions could incorporate multimodal content to enhance the realism of the simulation.
elements.sent-2411-383: While our asynchronous design helps to avoid bottlenecks, simulating millions of agents still requires several days to complete.
elements.sent-2411-384: Optimizing inference speed and improving the efficiency of database systems will be critical in reducing time and cost, making large-scale social simulations more feasible for widespread applications in the future.
elements.sent-2411-385: Our large-scale social simulation platform has the potential to serve as a foundational environment for other research.
elements.sent-2411-386: For instance, it can be used to evaluate the performance of novel recommendation systems or to train large language models (LLMs) with enhanced influence capabilities, using feedback from other agents in the network as a reward signal.
elements.sent-2411-387: The development and application of \GENSS provide valuable insights into complex social phenomena such as information propagation, group polarization, and herd effects.
elements.sent-2411-388: However, this also raises important ethical considerations.
elements.sent-2411-389: First, the replication of real-world social dynamics using large language model (LLM) agents introduces concerns regarding the fidelity and interpretation of the results.
elements.sent-2411-390: The risk of reinforcing biases, especially in areas related to misinformation or polarization, could exacerbate real-world issues if not properly managed.
elements.sent-2411-391: Researchers using \GENSS must be cautious in how these simulations influence public understanding or policy recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-393: While \GENSS is designed to replicate social media environments, the use of real-world data for training agents may introduce risks related to user anonymity and data security.
elements.sent-2411-394: Ensuring the ethical handling of any real-world datasets, including anonymization and consent, is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-395: Lastly, the scalability of \GENSS, while an asset for research, also presents potential dangers if misused.
elements.sent-2411-396: Large-scale agent-based models, particularly those that simulate millions of users, could be leveraged for unethical purposes such as manipulation of online discourse or misinformation campaigns.
elements.sent-2411-397: It is therefore essential to implement strict governance and ethical guidelines to prevent misuse of the simulator’s capabilities.

elements.sent-2411-409: It sends agents' information, such as user descriptions and their relationships, along with posts, to the Info Filter.
elements.sent-2411-410: The Info filter selects and pushes posts to agents through filtering algorithms, determining the visibility of content for each agent.
elements.subsection-2411-030: Simulating Large-Scale Misinformation Spreading on Platform X using \GENSS
elements.sent-2411-411: In the following subsection, we focus on Platform X (formerly Twitter), analyzing how both true and false information spread among millions of agents.
elements.sent-2411-412: Unlike Reddit, the action space on Twitter includes creating posts, repost, like post, dislike post, follow, create comment, like comment, and dislike comment.
elements.sent-2411-413: Additionally, we shift from the hot-score based recommendation system to an interest-based one.
elements.sent-2411-414: We select four news stories from official sources, covering health, technology, entertainment, and education.
elements.sent-2411-415: We then fabricate four pieces of misinformation that closely resemble the official news.
elements.sent-2411-416: Details are provided in Appendix~\ref{appendix:misinfo and truth pairs .}
elements.sent-2411-417: The experiment includes 196 core agents (with a large number of followers) and 1 million regular agents, as described in Section~\ref{sec:large-scale agents generation .}
elements.sent-2411-418: The simulation runs for 60 time steps, with an activation probability of 0.1 for core users and 0.01 for regular users.
elements.sent-2411-419: The experiment is conducted on 24 A100 GPUs within a week.
elements.paragraph-2411-018: Misinformation is more influential than the official news.
elements.sent-2411-420: To investigate the impact of official news and misinformation on the new posts generated by agents, we employ the TF-IDF-based approach~(Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency~\cite{christian2016single ).}
elements.sent-2411-421: Specifically, we calculate the cosine similarity between 8 news~(including 4 pairs of official and misinformation news) and a simulated set of 733824 posts generated by agents.
elements.sent-2411-422: A similarity threshold of 0.2 was set, with posts exceeding this threshold considered relevant to the target news.
elements.sent-2411-423: We then track the number of posts related to official news and misinformation at each time step, and the results are shown in the Figure~\ref{fig:misinfo .}
elements.sent-2411-424: As illustrated in the figure, for each topic, the number of posts related to misinformation consistently exceeds those related to official news.
elements.sent-2411-425: In the early stages, the number of posts related to both official news and misinformation increases rapidly due to the smaller number of relevant posts.
elements.sent-2411-426: However, over time, the number of posts declines quickly as posts on other topics gain more traction.
elements.sent-2411-427: Even in the later stages, posts related to misinformation maintain a higher level of activity, suggesting that misinformation has a more sustained influence.
elements.sent-2411-428: Furthermore, We visualize the new attention relationships formed during the simulation process.
elements.sent-2411-429: We find that these new connections exhibit a certain clustering phenomenon, where users tend to form concentrated clusters, with some forming densely connected central areas and others being more isolated.
elements.sent-2411-430: These subgraphs indicate the existence of complex relational networks, suggesting distinct communities within the overall structure.
